howItWorks.intro: Tire diameter is calculated from the metric tire size format (e.g. 225/60R16) using the section width, aspect ratio, and rim diameter.
rules: Sidewall height = section width (mm) × aspect ratio / 100
Overall diameter = (2 × sidewall height / 25.4) + rim diameter in inches
Revolutions per mile = 63,360 ÷ circumference in inches
Speedometer error = ((new diameter − original diameter) / original diameter) × 100%
howItWorks.outro: Most manufacturers recommend staying within 3% of the original tire diameter. Larger tires make your speedometer read slower than actual speed.

faq.question: How do I read a tire size like 225/60R16?
faq.answer: 225 is the section width in millimeters, 60 is the aspect ratio (sidewall height is 60% of width), R means radial construction, and 16 is the rim diameter in inches.

faq.question: How does tire size affect the speedometer?
faq.answer: A larger tire covers more ground per revolution, so your speedometer will read lower than your actual speed. A 3% larger tire means at an indicated 60 mph, you're actually going about 61.8 mph.

faq.question: What is the 3% rule for tire sizes?
faq.answer: Most vehicle manufacturers recommend staying within 3% of the original tire diameter. Going beyond this can affect speedometer accuracy, ABS, traction control, and may cause rubbing on fenders or suspension components.

faq.question: Does tire size affect fuel economy?
faq.answer: Yes. Larger tires are heavier and have more rolling resistance, which can decrease fuel economy by 1-2 MPG. Wider tires also increase aerodynamic drag slightly. However, the effect is usually small within the 3% range.

faq.question: Is the Tire Size Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

sections.title: What Is the Tire Size Calculator?
sections.body: The Tire Size Calculator is a free online tool designed for car owners and enthusiasts who need quick, accurate calculations in the automotive care and maintenance space. By entering your width, aspect ratio, rim, you get instant results including original diameter, new diameter, diameter difference. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting original diameter right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In automotive care and maintenance, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by car owners and enthusiasts worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to maintain your vehicle with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your width and need to find the right original diameter. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Width (mm)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your width — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: aspect ratio, rim, width, aspect ratio, rim. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your original diameter, new diameter, diameter difference. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your width before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both original diameter and new diameter — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to maintain your vehicle.
